perturbation.original_text: The Consultant acknowledges that any breach of the provisions of Section 1, 6 or 7 of this Agreement shall result in serious and irreparable injury to the Company for which the Company cannot be adequately compensated by monetary damages alone. The Consultant agrees, therefore, that, in addition to any other remedy the Company may have, the Company shall be entitled to enforce the specific performance of this Agreement by the Consultant and to seek both temporary and permanent injunctive relief (to the extent permitted by law) without the necessity of proving actual damages or posting a bond.
perturbation.changed_text: The Consultant acknowledges that any breach of the provisions of Section 1, 6 or 7 of this Agreement shall result in serious and irreparable injury to the Company for which the Company cannot be adequately compensated by monetary damages alone. The Consultant agrees, therefore, that, in addition to any other remedy the Company may have, the Company shall be entitled to seek both temporary and permanent injunctive relief (to the extent permitted by law) without the necessity of proving actual damages or posting a bond. The Company shall be entitled to enforce the specific performance of this Agreement by the Consultant only if the consultant agrees.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'only if the consultant agrees' to the part about specific performance, there is now a potential contradiction within the Remedies section. While the initial sentence suggests the company is entitled to specific performance, the added clause makes it contingent on the consultant's agreement, which undermines the enforceability of the specific performance clause. Specific Performance may only be used if monetary damages are inadequate. Here, that is being restricted.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 106, Section 2-716 outlines the right to specific performance in contracts. The added clause introduces an element of contingency, making the right to specific performance dependent on the consultant's agreement, contradicting the unconditional right that Massachusetts law may provide if monetary damages are inadequate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-716 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-716 Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin Section 2-716 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-716: Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin Section 2–716. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2) The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing, or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. In the case of goods bought for personal, family, or household purposes, the buyer's right of replevin vests upon acquisition of a special property, even if the seller had not then repudiated or failed to deliver. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 2-716 Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin :: 2016 Massachusetts General Laws :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Massachusetts General Laws 2023 Mass. Gen. Laws 2022 Mass. Gen. Laws 2021 Mass. Gen. Laws 2016 Mass. Gen. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2016 Massachusetts General Laws PART I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT TITLE XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-716 Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin MA Gen L ch 2 § 2-716 (2016) What's This? Section 2-716. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2) The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing, or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. In the case of goods bought for personal, family, or household purposes, the buyer's right of replevin vests upon acquisition of a special property, even if the seller had not then repudiated or failed to deliver. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition ('only if the consultant agrees') to the Company's right to specific performance, which contradicts the idea that specific performance is available when monetary damages are inadequate. The original text implies an entitlement to specific performance, while the modified text makes it subject to the consultant's agreement, undermining the enforceability of this remedy. The snippets, referencing Massachusetts General Law Chapter 106, Section 2-716, describe the buyer's (here, the Company's) right to specific performance, especially when goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. By making specific performance contingent on the consultant's agreement, the modified text weakens the Company's right to this remedy, potentially leading to non-compliance if monetary damages are inadequate, as the company may be blocked from specific performance by the consultant.

perturbation.original_text: All notices required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed effective upon personal delivery or upon deposit in the United States Post Office, by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, addressed to the other party at the address shown above, or at such other address or addresses as either party shall designate to the other in accordance with this Section 11.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed effective upon personal delivery. All notices may be sent by telepathy if the parties agree.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stated that notices must be written and effective on delivery. The modified text allows for telepathy as a means of sending notices. This introduces uncertainty and potential unenforceability. The original clause provided a clear, legally recognized method for delivering notices, whereas the addition of telepathy as an option introduces ambiguity and contradicts standard legal practices for providing notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added clause allows for telepathy, which is unenforceable and not legally valid. This contradicts common law principles, particularly the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 68, which emphasizes that a notification needs to be effectively communicated and received. This modification renders the original notice provision unenforceable because the notification medium is unclear.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces telepathy as a means of delivering notices, contradicting the legal requirement for effective communication and receipt as indicated by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 emphasizes that notice must be reasonably calculated to inform interested parties. Telepathy does not meet this standard because it is not a reasonably reliable or verifiable method of communication, rendering the notice provision unenforceable under established legal principles.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, whether written or oral, relating to the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, whether written or oral, relating to the subject matter of this Agreement. The parties agree that all oral side agreements will remain fully binding and may supersede any provision of this Agreement, as mutually agreed.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a statement that oral agreements remain fully binding and can supersede any provision of the written agreement, the 'entire agreement' clause is undermined. This creates ambiguity about what actually constitutes the full and binding agreement. This means that a party can claim reliance on an oral agreement that contradicts the written terms, making the contract's enforcement unpredictable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added sentence concerning oral agreements directly contradicts the Parol Evidence Rule, codified and explained in the Massachusetts Guide to Evidence § 101. The Parol Evidence Rule generally prevents parties from introducing evidence of prior or contemporaneous oral agreements to contradict or vary the terms of a fully integrated written contract. By stating that oral agreements can supersede the written agreement, the modification undermines the very purpose of the entire agreement clause and creates uncertainty.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly allows oral agreements to supersede written provisions, which contradicts the fundamental principle of contract law that written agreements should be binding and reliable. The Perturbation and Law explanations detail how this undermines the 'entire agreement' clause and violates the Parol Evidence Rule. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is somewhat unintelligible, the overall context, coupled with the other explanations, strongly supports the conclusion that the change introduces a legally significant contradiction by creating uncertainty and enforceability issues.
